# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Plan 9 from Outer Space (1959) - it's genuinely unlike anything else

**Score:** 43
**Body:** Ed Wood's alien invasion epic is famously chaotic, flying saucers on visible strings, scenes that contradict each other, dialogue that makes no sense and yet it has an almost irresistible energy that rewards an audience willing to just go with it.

It practically invented "so bad it's good," and holds up surprisingly well as a time capsule of 1950s paranoia and low-budget ambition. Bela Lugosi footage awkwardly spliced in, a pie-tin spaceship, a plot involving aliens trying to stop humanity by raising the dead. 

Wanted to drop it here for those who haven't heard of it. I'm gonna re-watch it this evening, anyone interested in a virtual watch party lmk!!! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I love the scene where Bela Lugosi walks through the cemetery and his cape is making the cheap prop gravestone flex and wobble.
  - so perfect 
2. I love doing a double feature of this and Ed Wood.
3. Oh, as a big so-bad-theyre-good fan, I have seen it several times.
4. 

High school plays have better acting than this movie. 

It's like if Ed Wood picked random people off the streets to act in the film and all the scenes were done in one take.


5. Check out double down by neil breen. 
6. This is crazy for me to see this suggestion because I just watched the X Files episode yesterday where Mulder and Scully are watching this movie for inspiration to solve a case. Definitely going to watch this movie now. 
7. Now watch the MST3K version.
8. In seattle, 35 years ago, I worked on a musical version of this film.  We used the same low tech props & scenery ( pie plate on a string).  Audiences howled !!!!
9. Excellent suggestion

## 2. please recommend some hidden gems on HBO or prime!

**Score:** 46
**Body:** i feel like i’ve seen everything good on HBO and just about everything on prime 😭 please give me some good hidden gem movie recommendations! if it helps, i enjoy horror, thriller, and drama movies the best. i’ve seen just about everything A24 movies that’s on HBO rn and i just finished rewatching the hunger games series. most recently i watched last night in soho. let me know what you like to watch!!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Night Of - great HBO mini series
2. The Leftovers and Duster, both on HBO.
  - The Leftovers is phenomenal. Pairs well with Carnivale.
3. Not enough people are watching Somebody Somewhere on HBO. It’s such a delightful show! 
4. Generation Kill on HBO
5. Not a movie. But “Undone” on Prime. 
  - Also The Patriot from the screenwriter for secret life of walter mitty.  Two seasons, wraps up nicely.  
6. On HBO The Outsider is chilling! I've watched that series several times. So good! Tokyo Vice also!

The first season of 30 Coins is pretty good. The second season was a disappointment though.

On Prime (but it might be through Shudder) check out NOS4A2  and Channel Zero
7. HBO - enlightened. Great series with Laura dern. Only ran 2 seasons.
8. Patriot. But it’s on Amazon.

## 3. Looking for solid, well-made, middlebrow movies of the 90s or early aughts that have been maybe unfairly forgotten

**Score:** 42
**Body:** Based on a random whim, I just recently watched 'Quiz Show', the 1994 movie directed by Robert Redford about the quiz show scandal of the 1950s (that's the one-line description of the movie, which tells me that the public knew way more about quiz show scandals back in the 1990s??). 

Anyway, it was a totally solid, well-made, interesting movie of the type they don't make anymore, and it made me want to watch more movies of that era that are really well done and entertaining but have been somewhat unfairly forgotten, because they are not flashy or envelope pushing (e.g. Pulp Fiction, Fight Club, Trainspotting) or huge money makers (e.g. Titanic, Forrest Gump, Jurassic Park), or cult classics (e.g. Slacker, the Big Lobowski) or just total rewatchables (e.g. Sleepless in Seattle, My best friend's wedding). 

Does anyone have any suggestions for movies that are maybe in that second or third echelon, a step or two beneath the rank of the well-known 90s classics? Something that you would recommend to someone who's familiar enough with 90s movies to know the above titles?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A Simple Plan (1998)
  - The Quick and the Dead (1995)
2. Sling Blade 
3. The Grifters (1990)
4. Michael Clayton (2007) was exactly what you are requesting for me. I somehow had not even heard of it.
5. I love Quiz Show and to be fair it was nominated for a best picture Oscar. Other movies I would recommend: 

Apollo 13 

A league of their own 

The Crow

Go

Talented Mr Ripley 

The negotiator  
6. My Blue Heaven. Written by Nora Ephron and based on the same mobster Henry Hill that Goodfellas was about. 
7. Some of my favorite '90s and '00s movies that weren't huge hits, that too many people don't know or remember:

Sneakers (1992)  
L.A. Confidential (1997)  
Dark City (1998)  
Dave (1993)  
Article 99 (1992)  
Dead Again (1991)  
Wild at Heart (1990)  
The Last Boy Scout (1991)  
Kiss Kiss Bang Bang (2006)  
Keeping the Faith (2000)  
The Game (1997)  
Devil with a Blue Dress (1995)  
Red Rock West (1993)  
The Rocketeer (1991)  
Wet Hot American Summer (2001) - kind of a cult classic, and it inspired prequel and sequel series on Netflix, but still mostly obscure.  
The Specials (2000) - a low-budget superhero mockumentary that was ahead of its time, coming out before superhero movies and mockumentaries became huge things in the 2000s.  Also, James Gunn wrote it.
8. I am not double checking release dates or popularity levels, so if some well known movies from the early 00's slip through... sorry, but here is my list from my hazy memory of being a movie guy teenager back in the 90's:

* Collateral
* Gattaca
* Equilibrium
* Being John Malcovich 
* Pitch Black
* Long Kiss Goodnight
* Underworld
* Anything with Nick Cage :-)
9. -The Jack Ryan movie series doesn't get as much love as other 90s action franchises. Maybe because it's more focused on politics than explosions. 

-Erin Brokovich

-The Station Agent

## 4. “Only Lover Left Alive” by Jim Jarmush. With Tilda Swinton, Tom Hiddleston, Jeffrey Wright, John Hurt & Mia Wasikowsska

**Score:** 37
**Body:** Only Lover Left Alive” directed by Jim Jarmush. Starring Tilda Swinton, Tom Hiddleston, Jeffrey Wright, John Hurt & Mia Wasikowsska Amazing performances. Great story. And the most romantic vampire movie ever made. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’ve watched this film at least 6 times and always am completely entranced by it.
  - This is also one of my favs of all time! Kinda a comfort movie, i love just throwing it on with some pizza or something and disappearing into it. Such a sick little take on vampires. I love that in this Tom and Mia's characters hate each other, But their characters in Crimson Peak (which is another amazing film imo) end up falling in love in a dark way, just a few years after Only Lovers
2. Only LoverS Left Alive" 

Fantastic movie. 
3. Anton Yelchin is also in this movie, him and Mia's chemistry was really cute 
4. One of my favorite Jarmusch films, hands down. Almost no one I mention it to has seen it, and they always thank me after watching. Such a gem!
5. Really good movie. 

If you like Jarmusch's style def check out Dead Man with Jonny Depp. Neil Young did the soundtrack. It's a slowburn and not for everyone, but if you like hypnotic fever-dream movies (it's a western, and in black and white) you might like this one.
6. Feel free to piss in the garden
7. Just watched this a few weeks ago and really enjoyed it
8. Thanks for the rec, op!
9. Oh heyyy... This was one of the films and ex of mine used to love and made me watch. Was fantastic! What a throwback!

## 5. Female Rage Movies

**Score:** 27
**Body:** Hi everyone! I've been loving movies lately that screams manic episodes and female rage, could anyone recommend movies that have similar essence with the following movies

1. Swallow (2020)  
2. Die My Love (2025)

9. Monster (2003)
10. If you can handle the blood, [I Spit On Your Grave (1978)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/I_Spit_on_Your_Grave) is a fantastic rape victim extracts revenge on her attackers movie. They made a remake (along with several sequals) in 2010 that was also really good

## 5. Thoughts on Willem Dafoe as Eric Masters in To Live and Die in L.A. (1985)? Was there a better ’80s movie villain?

**Score:** 264
**Body:** For me, To Live and Die in L.A. is one of the greatest crime films ever. I’m a huge William Friedkin fan, and his craft always elevated his movies to another level. I’d call him a “film mystic” because of his creative use of sound and his penchant for realism. Willem Dafoe’s portrayal of Eric Masters is a huge part of what makes the film so special. With the sharp haircut, the sleek style, and the cool swag, he carries himself like the criminal version of Crockett and Tubbs from Miami Vice.

What really makes Masters chilling is how he can flip from charming to cold-blooded killer in an instant. He comes off like a textbook sociopath. Masters also seems to see his criminal work as an extension of his artistic creation, but in the end it all goes up in flames just like his paintings. I don’t think there could’ve been a better casting choice. Without a doubt one of the most charismatic movie villains ever.

“18th century Cameroon. Yes, your taste is in your ass.”

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Clarence Boddicker
  - Bitches leave
    - I work for Dick Jones. DICK JONES!
  - ![gif](giphy|4LRkCWLi2MKOc|downsized)
2. Hans Gruber. /thread
3. The soundtrack to this film, by Wang Chung, is an underrated masterpiece.
  - I gotta rewatch. The whole film was great, and broke a lot of rules. The opening sequence was striking.
  - I used to listen to the tape I had of it while playing Lotus Esprit on my Amstrad home computer. As close as I could get to LA living in suburban Surrey. 
4. Freidkin said this is the best film he has ever made and the guy made epic films like Exorcist, French Connection and Sorcerer.
5. Love the sequence showing him forging the money.

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Marty Supreme and Sentimental Value as a serendipitous double feature

**Score:** 34
**Body:** I happened to watch both Sentimental Value and Marty Supreme last week in theaters and I've been pondering how viewing them as a in conversation adds further layers to both stories.

The two films are bookends of a male archetype: The incorrigible cad who survives on chutzpah and sheer charisma that lets them get away with things closed off to lesser mortals. And also, men driven by a higher artistic cause that they use to justify the neglect of human concerns they view as beneath them. The continual rewriting of their own stories to justify their actions and the constant need for myth making.

The ending of Marty Supreme asks us: What's next for Marty and, in a way, you can view Sentimental Value as answering that question. The world is so open to Marty as he embarks on this next chapter of parenthood but the message of both movies is that people never really change, really, deep down. 

Similarly, Sentimental Value is asking to what extent can we move on if we can't let go of the past and how much do we need to accommodate those who don't accommodate us. We get glimpses through Marty Supreme of the high flying days of Gustav why that lead to such bull headedness and inability to compromise.

But I think what's most interesting viewing them as bookends is that it leaves the gap in the middle for the viewer to fill in and live inside of. We're all able to create our own stories in this blank slate that helps mesh these two movies together.

I think it was a fascinating filmgoing experience to see both of these movies so closely together back to back and think about how they accidentally are speaking to each other.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Strangely, i always thought sentimental value and hamnet will make a nice double feature.. This is a fresh view, but I don't think you can put gustav and marty in the same scale, marty's asshole levels are at least several orders of magnitude higher than gustav to say the least
  - Are they, though? I think OP's comparison is on point. Even in his wiser old years, Gustav is dismissive and emotionally withholding, putting his art above everyone he loved or cared for. He's trying to reach out and re-establish connections, but only because he feels he is on death's doorstep. And he still treats his daughter and former colleague coldly. Cruelly telling her that her pain and anger makes her hard to love! He still gets by on charisma and competence without risking real intimacy.

One could easily imagine him being a Marty type in his youth, getting by on irreverent charm and artistic drive whatever the cost to everyone else. Just with more money, so he's not as desperate.
  - Funnily enough, this was the second time I saw Sentimental Value in a theater and the first time was on the same day I saw Hamnet. I like to go into movies as blind as I can so that was another serendipitous double.
2. I can’t take credit for this but I feel Paul Schrader nailed it when he suggested Marty Supreme and The Mastermind as a perfect double feature. Very similar character explorations with exact opposite film language styles. Would be fascinating to watch them back to back. 
3. Gustav Borg is implied to have had talent, and by the end of *SV* shows he still does.  Marty pretty consistently shoots himself in the foot with his arrogance.  (I can barely resist the urge to draw a connection to the actors….)

## 2. Director vs cinematographer: who is more responsible for the visual aspect of a film?

**Score:** 26
**Body:** It's something I've thought about over the years. I feel that the role of a cinematographer is sometimes overstated when it comes to how much input they have into the visual style of a film.

I am fairly certain - though feel free to correct me - that the director usually will not let the cinematographer decide the framing of a shot, or make them have much input into the coverage aspect of a specific scene (i.e. doing a dialogue scene as a master shot vs. shot-reverse-shot, how many close-ups/wide shots to use, etc.). Even things like using deep or shallow focus appear to me to fall under the director's discretion.

What appears to confirm my suspicion is that, generally speaking, directors tend to retain their visual styles across films shot by different cinematographers. Similarly, films shot by the same cinematographer but a different director can sometimes look wildly different from each other. The area where cinematographers tend to have the largest leeway in seems to be lighting (in the UK cinematographers even used to be called "Directors of Lighting" in the old days).

For those of you who have worked on actual Hollywood sets - have you found this to be true? Would you support switching to the old-school British nomenclature (i.e. bringing back the "Director of Lighting" title)?

Maybe I am completely off in my assessment of much sway a cinematographer tends to have on a given shoot?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Simple answer: Cinematographers are often also referred to as the *Director* of Photography.

Complex answer: Depends on the Director. Depends on the Cinematographer. Depends on the Producers.

Some directors choose to direct everyone including the cinematographer, some directors allow them far more freedom, some defer to them entirely. Sometimes producers step in and decide who makes which decisions.

Denis Villeneuve is the kind of director to lean on his DP far more often, and you can see subtle differences in visual style between most of his films (Roger Deakins) and the Dune films (Greg Fraser).
  - To add, some directors literally plan shot for shot what they want and how they want it.

Others show up on set and ask the cinematographer what they want and may even let them improvise on the spot.

At the end of the day, the director has to make the call in how they *direct* the crew.
2. I've worked with directors who have no idea about anything camera related. I've also worked with directors who know every single detail they want in a frame and how to capture it specifically. 

So, it depends and varies film to film. 
3. Yep, you're mostly right. Composition, movement, coverage is what generally should be attributed to the director and lighting, lens choices, texture to the cinematographer. Cinematographer will also do other stuff like choosing the gear. Of course they heavily influance eachother as they work together, and not everyone works the same, but that's the general rule of thumb. And yeah, watching different movies made by the same director but with different cinematographers is a good way to get some more insight.
4. I think the point you make about lighting is a good one. There are plenty of filmmakers who are very specific about coverage, lensing, and movement, but they still leave most lighting details to their cinematographer. 

But the bottom line is that it’s up to the director to decide how much authorship to take over the look. 

Any DP needs to be prepared to direct the broad coverage choices and make their own shotlists for directors who need that support. And they also need to be prepared to put all that aside when working for directors who have a specific vision and help them figure out how to execute those ideas. 
5. Then how do you explain dops whose style you can spot a mile off? Robert Richardson, Chivo, Bradford Young. Etc etc. There are many cinematographers who have a distinct visual style regardless of who they’re shooting for.

  

  - Because directors choose their DP's based on how they want the movie to look. Also, if you compare for example Casino and Shutter Island with Inglourious Basterds and OUATIH, the first two have clearly Scorsese's visual language and the latter two, Tarantino's. Lighting and texture of the image in all four is clearly Richardson's, but the scene coverage, blocking or camera movements are distinctive to their respective directors.
6. What’s the difference in prevalence of writer-directors today compared to twenty, and also forty years ago, and on and on?

It seems to me there’s a new paradigm today. Who better to realized a specific script than the person who wrote it? That seems to be pretty prevalent thinking. The cinematographer has attained a greater amount of recognition simultaneously to this writer-director trend. I think cinematographers on many projects today have more significance and perhaps autonomy. I can give many examples.

7. Sólo he visto una película en mí vida en la que el nombre del DOP, apareció por delante del director nada más acabar la película, Sturla Brandth Grøvlen en Victoria (2015), todo un homenaje por parte de los productores de la película, por el extraordinario esfuerzo de grabar una película durante dos horas y media sin descanso. 
Y según las notas del rodaje, se rodaron dos tomas, y se escogió la mejor de las dos. 
Un trabajo descomunal. 
8. It really depends / it’s more collaborative than saying it’s one or the other. The director has the final say, and will give notes on how things are looking, what their vision is, etc etc.

However, cinematographers are so honed on their specific craft, that they may take the directors vision, and offer some methods of execution. Then maybe the director sees that and goes, oh let’s try it again but tweak this.

Some directors choose a cinematographer because of their specific style, and give them more reigns on how it all looks / is executed.

I’m an Art Director and my process is much like this. Sometimes I have an idea that is crystal clear and I need someone who can do it. Sometimes I have direction on overall tone, mood, angles, color, etc, but need a cinematographer to weigh in and offer their perspective.

## 3. The Battle of Chile (1975)

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Directed by Patricio Guzmán

Patricio Guzmán's The Battle of Chile is a direct record of a complex political process and a society seemingly divided. Through the trilogy, he reconstructs the months leading up to the 1973 coup that ended Salvador Allende's government and ushered in one of the darkest periods in Chilean history.

Each part focuses on different moments of the conflict. The first (The Insurrection of the Bourgeoisie) depicts the atmosphere before the 1973 parliamentary elections and the social polarization gripping the country. Through street interviews and footage of demonstrations, we see how different social classes perceived Allende's government, some with hope, others with distrust or open opposition. We also witness how various factions of the political opposition began to do everything possible to sabotage Allende's government.

The second part (The Coup d'Etat) focuses on the military coup itself, and the footage was recorded as the events unfolded. The camera becomes a direct witness to the collapse of democracy, and we see confrontations and political speeches that reflect the level of tension in the country.

In the third part (The Power of the People), the focus shifts to the organization of workers and other social movements during the Popular Unity government. Through assemblies, meetings, and testimonies, the documentary shows how various sectors of the population attempted to actively participate in the country's political transformation and, despite the obstacles posed by the political opposition, the workers did everything possible to support President Allende. This part helps us understand the expectations and aspirations of many citizens who saw an opportunity for change in this process before the coup.

After the military coup, much of the team had to leave Chile to continue their work. The filmed material managed to leave the country and was edited abroad with international support. During this process, the film became an act of cultural and political resistance. Furthermore, the fate of some of its collaborators, such as the disappearance of photographer Jorge Müller, reminds us of the severity of the repression that followed the coup.

In all three parts, Guzmán doesn't try to hide his political perspective, but neither does he impose a rigid interpretation of the events. Throughout the film, the viewer is invited to reflect for themselves. Although the main objective is to depict historical events, the images clearly convey the emotions of those who lived through that moment, such as the hope of those who supported the left-wing political project, the frustration of its opponents, and the fear that spread as the crisis provoked by the same opponents and the United States deepened.

More than 50 years after the coup, the question of how we, as a society, reached such a breaking point remains relevant. The Battle of Chile is a historical tool and reminds us of the importance of keeping historical memory alive, especially where the past continues to influence the present.

[Letterboxd (review in Spanish)](https://boxd.it/dsR9Dt)

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. Question(s) about The Secret Agent

**Score:** 10
**Body:** I feel like I'm not clear enough regarding what is actually happening in this film.

* Is the film examining the corruption and negligence that pervaded Brazil during the military dictatorship, the abuses of the military dictatorship itself, or both? It seems like a lot of the violence we see and hear about is the result of civilian crime (the corpse in the car park, Geisa's fiance killing her etc.), or are we supposed to understand that these events are actually instances of state-sanctioned violence?
* I also don't fully understand the whole business with Ghirotti. Who exactly is he? Why does he want to gut Armando's research department? How and why does he have the power to make people disappear? (Also, what is it about his conduct at the dinner with Armando and Fatima that angers Fatima so much? It can't just be the "you started as his secretary" comment, can it?)
* What is the significance of the scene at the ID office with the woman who comes to make a deposition? How does it relate to the film's themes/ideas about what was going on in Brazil in the '70s?
* What exactly is the Angolan couple's situation? I can't tell if they're refugees from the Angolan Civil War or if they're living at Dona Sebastiana's because of activities they've got involved in since coming to Brazil.

I'm not sure if these queries/uncertainties are because I didn't watch the film attentively enough or because the film assumes certain knowledge on the part of its audience that I don't have. Any help with understanding it better would be much appreciated.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Its showing how a pro ”law & order” military dictatorship does nothing to address the crime of murder, has corrupt officials that prey on civilians, and can be recruited by thr wealthy to selectively “enforce the law” against people who get in the way of the capitalist class in Brazil. 

Ghirotti also sits on the board of a ~~petroleum~~ electric consortium, his main job. He’s using his access to government funded sciences to 1) capture Armando’s electric car battery patents and take them off the market so they wont compete ~~in time with gas powered cars~~ *with Ghirotti’s own private interests he’s pursuing through Electrobras (so an even more hypocritical parallel to Armando)* and 2) collapse the range of scientific grants funding research in the northern part of the country, which he describes in racist terms as a cultural and social backwater thats not even really Brazil, but more of a resource range for “real Brazil.”

At dinner, theres all sorts of euphemistic language and intonation and him and his son are telling Armando “wow youre some bohemian libtard using the public education system to make money by patenting inventions (that i covet) and im gonna defund your department tomorrow so its best if you play along, also its low key gross you married some black chick, you see how these people are.”

This goes back to the idea of the super rich participating in government just so that they can use institutional power to suppress anything that threatens their business interests. 

The deposition is a super wealthy woman who sent one of her live-in maids out to run an errand and while the woman (maid) was out her son was left unattended and was run over (iirc by the rich woman, but in their driveway either way) and the maid came home to find her son dead and missing and flipped out when no one would tell her what happened (which made the rich lady feel “unsafe”. 
So the rich lady paid the police to rent an empty buildings and hire a temporary secretarial staff to create “a court office” where the rich lady could give her deposition but also intentionally not telling the woman whose child was killed so that she wouldnt show up to the hearing and give her own statement. But the woman finds out anyway. 

But again, you have corrupt courts and cops literally paying a room full of people to pretend to type so they can have a mock building to take a single perjured statement from an ultra-wealthy person so she can feel “safe”

They are refugees from Angola. We dont know what theyre involved in specifically as a foil because the husband, having seen how things can turn on a dime back home, wont divulge anything that could compromise their safety- in contrast to the brazilians who are more hopeful that they can still be more open as long as theyre “careful” (which we eventually see is up and down disasteously insufficient when the hired goons come into play)

Edited: my bad Ghirotti is an electrofascist. Not a petrofascist. Corrections above. *Perdão*
2. 1. Both. Dictatorships in general facilitate corruption and negligence, because checks and balances are gone. They are all one and the same.
2. Girotti is the chair (or part of the board) of the state electricity company,a s well owns his own companies in the sector. Think of what you know about oligarchs: individuals in charge of state enterprise become all powerful to influence politics without any repercussions. He wants to gut Armando's research department because they were doing research on EV batteries, which would be patented by the university, and Girotti used his connections (probably as a chair from the state electricity company) to invest on EV batteries prototypes from Canada. If the university launches their prototype, Girotti loses a bunch of money.
3. It is something that still exists in Brazil (and perhaps the world in general, more or less?) but much more pronounced during the corruption-ridden dictatorship times: rich people having their own way, oppressing the poor, and not facing consequences. The rich lady used her power/connections (many rich people back then could ask someone in the government for "favors") to use state offices for their own gain in absurd ways, such as making an ID office into a police station for one morning, so just she could make her botched statement in peace and away from the attention of the media.
4. Most probably they are political refugees from the Angolan Civil War, which much like Dona Sebastiana herself was doing during Mussolini's Italia, they were doing something against Salazar iron fist colonization policy on Angola. (Edit: I think the later makes more sense than the Civil War in itself, because the woman mentions she made a case of never boarding a plane with Portugal flag on it)
3. The film definitely assumes the audience has some knowledge, but at the same time the lack of explanation didn’t bother me all that much because I put myself into the shoes of the students studying the tapes and newspapers. I as an audience member am working with an incomplete story in the same way they are, piecing together what I can from what the film chooses to show. I don’t understand it all, yet get invested in Armando’s struggles all the same.
4. Is it all oppressive state corruption or civilian chaos and crime writ large — I suppose it’s a mix.

Ghirotti — seems he’s an industrialist who’s been given a state sanction to do what he’s doing re: the university.

He hires hitmen out of his own pocket. Is there more to it than that?

The couple feel that dining with him at all is more or less an affront. It escalates from there. Obviously he’s being very racist.

The deposition — represents elitism at work. Seems to illustrate a situation which feels like 1%ers with the power, and an insurrectionary mood pervasive among the lower orders. A human interest case like this seems like something that easily excites such feeling.

The Angolan couple — yeah I suppose it’s most easy to infer that it’s the Angolan civil war from which they’re refugees.

  - I would also add that Armando’s research is mainly about electric engines and cars. I don’t think its a stretch to say that Ghirotti likely rubs shoulders with big oil and his research can be seen as a threat. Thats why members of his team start disappearing in the first place. 
5. Your last point is very important. I've seen all his films. I research the history of Brazil and these places after watching these movies. I've learned so much about Brazilian history through this research. A large chunk of his audience is Brazilians, who will be familiar with these historical events. Knowing this history certainly makes it easier to understand what's going on in the film. Being familiar with his earlier works is also very helpful. He uses a lot of the same actors and reuses a lot of structural elements. Do you need to know all this history to watch this film? No. Does it help? Yes. Would it be worthwhile to learn some of this history before rewatching the film? Absolutely. All his films are very solid and worthy of rewatching.
6. The only part I didn't understand was why Ghiroti hated Armando so much. I got that he was racist against northerners and wanted to shut down his academic research but it seemed like Ghiroti succeeded in doing that with no difficulty at all and Armando couldn't really do anything to stop him. But when he was hiring the hitmen Ghiroti seemed to have a very personal hatred for Armando 
  - You have to keep in mind Ghirotti is a *literal* fascist oligarch. Ghirotti was in bed with the dictatorship and the industrialists of Brazil hid their crimes under anti-communism, fighting subversives. Hence the title: “secret agent” right?


To someone like him, Armando is not just “slumming it” in an interracial family, he’s a gender traitor with long hair, a strong wife, and he treats his female academic peers with enough deference and respect that they can speak directly to and contradict Ghirotti. 

In that context, the night before the meeting in the diner where Ghirotti’s son gets hit by Armando’s wife and then she reads him and Ghirotti to filth in front of everyone. 

These were peasants speaking above their station to disrespect a Lord, in Ghirotti’s eyes. 

Not only did they humiliate him and walk away, at first, but then Armando proceeded to refuse to be the patsy to dismantle his own department. Then he even cheekily says something to Ghirotti like “ya im a socialist.”
Multiple slaps in the face to someone who barely wipes their own ass. 


Also Armando implies the entire dept was liquidated, which likely means Ghirotti had had to arrange murders for like a dozen different people over the years. Defeating Armando had become all consuming obsession to Ghirotti. 

## 5. Mirror Characters in Opening Night (1977)

**Score:** 5
**Body:** Lots of films have characters that mirror each other, it's one of the most classic, archetypal tropes of storytelling. Darth Vader is a dark reflection of Luke Skywalker, we have Neil and Vincent in Heat or Alma and Elisabet in Persona. But, I think John Cassavetes' Opening Night has the most extensive use of mirror characters I've ever seen in a film ever.

Opening Night traps us within a house of mirrors: the protagonist is surrounded by characters who reflect different aspects of herself, the conflicts on stage reflect those happening behind the scenes, almost every character in the film is mirrored by another in some way, the locations mirror each other and even we, the audience, have a mirror image in the film.

Correct me if I'm wrong but I don't think any film uses mirror characters to the same extent.

I discuss this at greater length here: [youtu.be/ZUa0AwaWX7E](https://youtu.be/ZUa0AwaWX7E)
